summary: The Hundred Years' War (1337-1453) was a series of conflicts fought between the ruling houses of England (Plantagenet) and France (Valois) over the French crown, sparked when Edward III of England pressed a claim to the French throne through his mother Isabella of France after the senior Capetian line died out. Punctuated by major English victories at Crecy (1346), Poitiers (1356, where King John II of France was captured), and Agincourt (1415, celebrated in Shakespeare's Henry V), the war also saw the devastating chevauchee raids of Edward the Black Prince, the Jacquerie peasant revolt, and the intervention of Joan of Arc, who broke the Siege of Orleans in 1429 before being captured and burned at the stake. The war finally turned in France's favor under Charles VII, who defeated the English at the Battle of Castillon (1453); it was formally ended by the 1475 Treaty of Picquigny under Louis XI.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How the war is identified in giveaway lines: a very long medieval conflict between England and France lasting from 1337 to 1453, encompassing the Battles of Crecy, Poitiers, and Agincourt and the intervention of Joan of Arc, whose history is chiefly recorded in Jean Froissart's Chronicles.

clues.clue: A very long medieval war fought between England and France from 1337 to 1453, including the Battles of Crecy, Poitiers, and Agincourt
examples: name this long war fought between England and France that included the Battle of Agincourt
For 10 points, name this really long war between England and France.

clues.clue: Its history is chiefly recorded in Jean Froissart's Chronicles, considered the best primary source for its events
examples: This war, whose history is detailed in Jean Froissart's Chronicles, began with a naval battle at Sluys.
The best primary sources for events during the Hundred Years War come from this French chronicler.

clues.clue: Its tide was later turned by the intervention of Joan of Arc, the "Maid of Orleans"
examples: During this war, Henry V won the Battle of Agincourt, while his opponents were inspired by Joan of Arc.
Henry V's victory at Agincourt was during this long conflict between England and France whose tide was later turned by the Maid of Orleans, Joan d'Arc.

clues.clue: Prompt-worthy alternate names for its phases include the Edwardian War/Phase, the Caroline War, and the Lancastrian War
examples: Answer: Hundred Years' War [or Guerre de Cent Ans; accept Edwardian phase until "Edward" is read, even though that is named for Edward III; accept Caroline or Lancastrian phase]

clues.clue: A later, unrelated "Second Hundred Years' War" nickname is sometimes applied to the Anglo-French conflicts of 1689-1815, funded in part by Britain's excise tax
examples: The excise tax helped Britain win the second of these conflicts against France, which lasted from 1689 to 1815. Henry V won the Battle of Agincourt during the first conflict of this name, which also lasted a very long time.

works.name: Causes & Outbreak: Succession Dispute and the Battle of Sluys
works.description: The war grew out of a succession dispute after the senior line of the Capetian dynasty failed to produce a male heir: Edward III of England, whose claim to the French throne came through his mother Isabella of France (a former lover of Roger Mortimer), disputed the crown with Philip VI of Valois amid confusion over Salic law. Philip VI's confiscation of English-held Aquitaine (gained by England when Eleanor of Aquitaine married Henry II) made Edward III his vassal and helped spark the conflict, which formally opened with the English naval victory at the Battle of Sluys (1340) near the Scheldt estuary.

clues.clue: Grew out of a succession dispute between Philip VI and Edward III after the senior line of the Capetian dynasty failed to produce a male heir
examples: That king, John the Good, was ransomed in the Treaty of Bretigny after being captured at the Battle of Poitiers during this war, which grew out of a succession dispute between Philip VI and Edward III.
Edward III's claim was strengthened by the fact that the senior line of the Capetian dynasty failed to produce a male heir, leading to this lengthy conflict between England and France.

clues.clue: Edward III's claim to the French throne came through his mother, Isabella of France, a former lover of Roger Mortimer; confusion over Salic law fueled the dispute
examples: The English claim to the French throne during the Hundred Years' War came from Edward III's mother of this name, who was a lover of Roger Mortimer.

As a result of widespread suspicion of women in power after the Tour de Nesle affair and confusions over Salic law, this English king, the son of Isabella, disputed the throne of France with Philip VI.

clues.clue: Philip VI's confiscation of English-held Aquitaine, gained by England when Eleanor of Aquitaine married Henry II, made Edward III a vassal and helped spark the war; just after pardoning Roger Mortimer's widow Joan, Edward III instigated the conflict by claiming to be the rightful heir
examples: The war was sparked in part by Philip VI's confiscation of English holdings in this French region, which made Edward III his vassal. England gained this territory when a woman from here named Eleanor married Henry II.
Just after pardoning Joan, Edward III instigated this long conflict with France after claiming that he was the rightful heir to the French throne.

clues.clue: The war formally began with an English naval victory at the Battle of Sluys, fought near the Scheldt estuary off the coast of Flanders
examples: This war, whose history is detailed in Jean Froissart's Chronicles, began with a naval battle at Sluys.
Briefly suspended by the Peace of Bretigny, this war began at the Battle of Sluys.
One side had its navy destroyed at the Battle of Sluys and suffered a decisive defeat on St. Crispin's Day.

clues.clue: Philip VI, the sixth French monarch of that name, earlier defeated a Flemish uprising at the Battle of Cassel but then suffered defeats against the English at Sluys and Crecy
examples: The sixth French monarch of this name defeated a Flemish uprising at the Battle of Cassel, but was less successful against the English in the Hundred Years' War, suffering defeats at Sluys and Crecy.

works.name: Battle of Crecy (1346)
works.description: An early English victory in which Edward the Black Prince (Edward III's son) helped defeat the French, who had hired Genoese crossbowmen to fight for them; the blind King John of Bohemia was killed in the battle, and the English victor subsequently adopted his crest of three ostrich feathers. The battle established the effectiveness of the English longbow against French cavalry and crossbow mercenaries.

clues.clue: Edward the Black Prince achieved a major victory here, part of a pair of triumphs alongside Poitiers
examples: Edward the Black Prince achieved major victories during this conflict at the Battles of Crecy and Poitiers.
One man with this name led English troops at the battles of Crecy and Poitiers and is known as the "Black Prince."

clues.clue: The French hired Genoese crossbowmen to fight against Edward III here
examples: Name this conflict during which the French hired Genoese crossbowmen to fight against Edward III at the Battle of Crecy.

clues.clue: The blind King John of Bohemia died in this battle, and the English victor subsequently adopted his crest of three ostrich feathers
examples: The blind John of Bohemia died at one battle in this war.
In a battle of this war, the blind King John of Bohemia was killed, leading the victor to adopt his crest of three ostrich feathers.

clues.clue: Recurs alongside Poitiers and Agincourt as one of the war's three most-cited battles
examples: The War of the Breton Succession was an early side-act in this larger Anglo-French conflict, known for the Battles of Crecy and Agincourt.
The Jacquerie and the Battle of Poitiers both took place during this rather long war between England and France, which saw other notable engagements at Agincourt and Crecy.

works.name: Battle of Poitiers (1356) & the Treaty of Bretigny
works.description: Edward the Black Prince captured King John II "the Good" of France at this 1356 battle; John yielded himself to the banished French knight Denis of Morbeke, who was by then fighting for England. John's capture ended the war's Edwardian phase and led to the humiliating Treaty of Bretigny (1360), which ransomed him for three million ecus, 40 hostages (including Louis I, Duke of Anjou, who later dishonorably escaped captivity), and large territories including Guyenne and Gascony; the resulting political instability helped spark the Jacquerie peasant revolt.

clues.clue: Edward the Black Prince captured King John II "the Good" of France at this battle; John yielded himself to the banished French knight Denis of Morbeke, who was fighting for England
examples: That king, John the Good, was ransomed in the Treaty of Bretigny after being captured at the Battle of Poitiers during this war, which grew out of a succession dispute between Philip VI and Edward III.

Before being ransomed, John was captured by Edward the Black Prince at this battle. Jean Froissart reports that, at this battle, the king yielded himself to the banished French knight Denis of Morbeke, who was by then fighting for England.

clues.clue: John II's capture and ransom here ended the war's "Edwardian phase"
examples: The Battle of Poitiers and the Treaty of Bretigny ended the Edwardian phase of this lengthy medieval conflict between England and France.

clues.clue: The resulting Treaty of Bretigny ransomed John II for three million ecus, 40 hostages, and territories including Guyenne and Gascony; hostage Louis I, Duke of Anjou, later dishonorably escaped captivity, prompting John II to surrender himself as a replacement prisoner
examples: Name this payment set by the Treaty of Bretigny at three million ecus, 40 hostages, and such large territories as Guyenne and Gascony. One hostage, Louis I, Duke of Anjou, dishonored this payment by escaping from captivity.
During this war, one ruler gave himself up as a prisoner after his son dishonorably escaped from being held captive in place of him.

clues.clue: The resulting political instability from John II's capture here helped spark the Jacquerie peasant revolt
examples: The Jacquerie was partly prompted by political instability after the French defeat at the Battle of Poitiers during this wider conflict against England.
The Jacquerie broke out after the capture of John II of France at the Battle of Poitiers in this war.

works.name: The Jacquerie (1358)
works.description: A three-week peasant revolt in northern France, named for a derogatory nickname for peasants ("Jacques Bonhomme"), led by Guillaume Cale (called "Jack Goodfellow" by chronicler Jean Froissart). Erupting after John II's capture and ransom left the countryside in political chaos, the rebels set fire to manor houses and killed noble families before Charles the Bad of Navarre tricked and imprisoned Cale and crushed the revolt at the Battle of Mello.

clues.clue: Led by Guillaume Cale, nicknamed "Jack Goodfellow" (or "Jacques Bonhomme") by chronicler Jean Froissart
examples: Froissart reports that Jack Goodfellow, or Guillaume Cale, led the Jacquerie uprising during this war.
This fourteenth-century aristocrat referred to the rebellion's leader as Jack Goodfellow.
William Cale led the Jacquerie revolt during this war after John II was captured at the Battle of Poitiers and ransomed.

clues.clue: Charles the Bad of Navarre tricked and imprisoned Cale before crushing the revolt at the Battle of Mello
examples: During this conflict, the Battle of Mello ended a peasant revolt led by a man often called Jack Goodfellow.
Charles the Bad put down this rebellion and tortured its leader at the Battle of Mello.

clues.clue: The revolt's name derives from peasants' padded tunics ("jacques") and a derogatory nickname for its leader
examples: Name this peasant revolt that took place in northern France during the summer of 1358. This rebellion takes its name from peasants' padded tunics, as well as from the derogatory first name given to its leader.

clues.clue: Its motivations paralleled those of the English Peasants' Revolt of 1381, led by Wat Tyler
examples: The motivations of the Jacquerie were similar to those of the English Peasants Revolt of 1381, which was led by this man.

works.name: War of the Breton Succession
works.description: A proxy war fought within the wider conflict over control of Brittany, pitting the House of Blois against the House of Montfort after the death of Duke John III sparked a succession dispute. English forces backing the Montforts, aided by Sir John Chandos (a founding Knight of the Garter and mastermind of the English victories at Crecy and Poitiers), ultimately triumphed at the Battle of Auray, though Brittany later swore homage to France regardless. The war featured the Combat of the Thirty, a celebrated example of medieval chivalry in which Robert Bemborough was killed.

clues.clue: A succession dispute in the House of Dreux, following Duke John III's death, pitted the Montfort family against the Blois family for control of Brittany
examples: A succession dispute in the House of Dreux led to a proxy war involving the Montfort and Blois families.
The death of Duke John III of this region sparked a succession war that marked one of the early phases of the Hundred Years War.

clues.clue: English forces backing the Montforts, aided by Sir John Chandos (a founding Knight of the Garter and mastermind of the victories at Crecy and Poitiers), won at the Battle of Auray, though Brittany then swore homage to France
examples: The Montforts eventually won after victory at the Battle of Auray. They were supported in that battle by this Englishman, founding member of the Order of the Garter and mastermind of the victories of Crecy and Poitiers.
Name this region, the site of the Battle of Auray, where English and Montfort forces defeated French forces. Unfortunately for the English, this region then proceeded to swear homage to France.

clues.clue: The war featured the Combat of the Thirty, a celebrated feat of medieval chivalry in which Robert Bemborough was killed
examples: Robert Bemborough was killed in an engagement in this war that is often heralded as the prime example of medieval chivalry, the Combat of the Thirty.

clues.clue: Described as "an early side-act" or "early phase" of the wider Hundred Years' War
examples: The War of Breton Succession was an early side-act in this larger Anglo-French conflict, known for the Battles of Crecy and Agincourt.
The War of the Breton Succession was a phase of this larger, very lengthy conflict that began when the British monarch Edward III instituted a claim to the French throne.

works.name: Mercenaries, Chevauchees & Wartime Society
works.description: The war was marked by chevauchee raids — fast, devastating cavalry raids on the countryside (a tactic earlier used by the Spanish) carried out by Edward III's troops and, most famously, by Edward the Black Prince — and by demobilized mercenary "free companies" (routiers) that turned to brigandry, including the pillaging "late-comers" and clothes-stealing "Flayers of Dead Men" led by Rodrigo de Villandrando, the "Emperor of Pillagers," and a siege expert nicknamed the Archpriest who personally confessed to the Pope after extorting villages of the Queen of Naples. Italian condottiere John Hawkwood led many war veterans in the mercenary White Company, while knight Geoffroi de Charny's participation in the war informed his influential Book of Chivalry. The war's brutalizing effects on individuals included the notorious later serial killer Gilles de Rais, who as a young commander helped relieve a besieged Armagnac-aligned city, and the judicial duel in which Jean de Carrouges killed his former comrade-in-arms Jacques le Gris.

clues.clue: Chevauchee raids — fast, devastating cavalry raids on the countryside, a tactic earlier used by the Spanish — were carried out by Edward III's troops, ravaging the French countryside most notably under Edward the Black Prince
examples: One side in this war made use of a raiding tactic earlier utilized by the Spanish that was known as the chevauchee.
This French term refers to the fast, devastating raids on the countryside that were carried out by Edward III's troops during the Hundred Years' War.
the French countryside was ravaged by Edward the Black Prince's chevauchee raids during what very long late medieval war?

clues.clue: Demobilized mercenary "free companies" (routiers) turned to brigandry, including the pillaging "late-comers" and "Flayers of Dead Men," led by Rodrigo de Villandrando, the "Emperor of Pillagers"
examples: During this war, pillaging "late-comers" and clothes-stealing "flayers" led by the "Emperor of Pillagers," Rodrigo de Villandrando, were among demobilized free companies that turned to brigandry.
During this war, groups of unemployed mercenaries such as the Flayers of Dead Men and the Routiers pillaged the countryside.

clues.clue: A siege expert nicknamed the Archpriest for his former vocation personally confessed to the Pope after extorting villages subject to the Queen of Naples
examples: During this war, a siege expert nicknamed the Archpriest for his former vocation personally confessed to the Pope after extorting villages subject to the Queen of Naples.

clues.clue: Italian condottiere John Hawkwood led many English and French war veterans in the mercenary White Company
examples: One of these people named John Hawkwood led many English and French Hundred Years' War veterans in the White Company. These mercenary captains fought various wars in Italy.

clues.clue: Knight Geoffroi de Charny's participation in the war informed his influential Book of Chivalry
examples: The practice of furusiyah likely influenced the Book of Chivalry, written after Geoffroi de Charny's participation in this Europe-based conflict. Jean Froissart discussed chivalry during this conflict in the Chronicles.

clues.clue: Later notorious serial killer Gilles de Rais fought in the war, at one point helping relieve a siege of an Armagnac-aligned city
examples: In this war, serial killer Gilles de Rais helped relieve a siege of an Armagnac-aligned city.

clues.clue: After fighting together in the war, knight Jacques le Gris was killed by his former comrade Jean de Carrouges in a high-profile judicial duel
examples: After fighting together in this war, Jacques le Gris was killed by Jean de Carrouges in a high-profile judicial duel.

works.name: Battle of Agincourt (1415)
works.description: Henry V's celebrated victory, fought on Saint Crispin's Day, in which English and Welsh longbowmen (used by up to 80% of English troops, with bows often made of yew wood) destroyed the cavalry of Charles d'Albret after a charge across muddy terrain that catastrophically slowed the French horses; the absent Charles VI's army was routed. Shakespeare later dramatized the battle in Henry V, including the king's St. Crispin's Day speech ("we happy few, we band of brothers").

clues.clue: Fought on Saint Crispin's Day; commemorated by Shakespeare's Henry V, including the king's speech beginning "we happy few, we band of brothers"
examples: One side's heavy use of longbows in this conflict secured a pivotal victory at a battle fought on Saint Crispin's Day.
Charles d'Albret was killed during a battle in this war that was preceded by the St. Crispin's Day speech.
Name this event. In a Shakespeare history play, the title king addresses "we happy few, we band of brothers" in the St Crispin's Day speech just before this historical event.

clues.clue: A cavalry charge across muddy terrain catastrophically slowed the French forces under Charles d'Albret, who was killed in the battle, allowing English longbowmen to destroy them
examples: A later battle in this war was fought on muddy terrain that catastrophically slowed Charles D'Albert's cavalry.
A cavalry charge across a muddy field during this war was repulsed by one side's longbowmen at a battle won by Henry V.

clues.clue: English and Welsh longbowmen, used by up to 80% of English troops and often wielding bows made of yew wood, decimated the French cavalry here
examples: Name this weapon used by up to 80% of English troops against Charles d'Albret's forces at the Battle of Agincourt. This weapon was often made from yew wood.
One battle that was part of this conflict resulted in French cavalry being decimated by Welsh longbowmen at Agincourt.

clues.clue: Longbow-wielding archers destroyed the army of the absent King Charles VI here
examples: Name this battle fought on Saint Crispin's Day. Longbow-wielding archers proved to be crucial in destroying the absent Charles VI's army.

clues.clue: Henry V's victory here was followed by his marriage to Catherine of Valois and the 1420 Treaty of Troyes
examples: A few years after his triumph at Agincourt, Henry V married Catherine of Valois and this treaty, signed in a French city in 1420, established that Henry's heirs would inherit the French throne.

works.name: Joan of Arc & the Siege of Orleans (1429)
works.description: Joan of Arc, a peasant girl and Catholic saint, led the French army in lifting the English siege of Orleans in 1429, a major turning point that allowed her to help crown Charles VII at Reims; she was later captured by Burgundian forces, handed to the English, and burned at the stake, ostensibly for the heresy of cross-dressing.

clues.clue: Joan of Arc, a peasant girl who later helped crown Charles VII at Reims, led the French army in lifting this English siege in 1429
examples: Its major turning points included Henry V's victory at Agincourt and the siege of Orleans, which was broken by Joan of Arc in 1429.
Name this conflict between Britain and France which turned in France's favor after the entrance of Joan of Arc during the Siege of Orleans.

clues.clue: Joan of Arc was later captured by Burgundian forces, handed to the English, and burned at the stake for the heresy of cross-dressing
examples: Joan of Arc helped fend off the siege of Orleans during this conflict.
She's no pope, but Joan of Arc was burned at the stake for cross-dressing during this lengthy war broadly fought between England and France during the 14th and 15th centuries.

clues.clue: Joan of Arc's intervention after the siege turned the tide of the war against the English in its later phases
examples: The War of Breton Succession was part of this larger and very long conflict between France and England. In its later phases, the forces of Joan of Arc turned the tide against the English.
Both the Jacquerie and the English Peasants' revolt took place during this lengthy war between France and England. Joan of Arc participated in the Siege of Orleans during this war.

clues.clue: The longbow remained in use by English armies from 1337 to 1453, throughout the war, until Joan of Arc helped lead French armies to victory after the siege
examples: The longbow was used by English armies throughout much of this war from 1337 to 1453, during which the English tried to subjugate France. Joan of Arc helped lead French armies in this war after the Siege of Orleans.

works.name: Valois Kings: Charles V, Charles VI & Isabeau of Bavaria
works.description: Charles V "the Wise," who appointed Bertrand du Guesclin as constable, won a naval victory at the Battle of La Rochelle (with Castilian help) and was known for the marauding ecorcheurs who harassed French peasants during his reign. His son Charles VI "the Mad," who believed he was made of glass, presided over a court marked by disaster — a masquerade ball thrown by his queen, Isabeau of Bavaria, ended when a torch set costumed dancers ablaze (the Bal des Ardents) — and his reign's instability, worsened by the Burgundian-Armagnac civil war, contributed to the war's darkest years for France.

clues.clue: Charles V "the Wise" appointed Bertrand du Guesclin as constable and won a naval victory at the Battle of La Rochelle with Castilian help; marauding ecorcheurs harassed French peasants during his reign
examples: Name this king who appointed Bertrand du Guesclin as constable. This king's navy and a Castilian force under Ambrosio Boccanegra defeated the Earl of Pembroke's navy at the Battle of La Rochelle.
Marauders known as ecorcheurs harassed French peasants during this conflict.

clues.clue: Charles VI "the Mad" believed he was made of glass, and a masquerade ball thrown by his queen, Isabeau of Bavaria, ended when a torch set costumed dancers on fire (the Bal des Ardents)
examples: A ruler during this war suffered from the glass delusion and held a party at which some knights dressed as wild men were accidentally set on fire.

At a masquerade thrown by this queen, a torch on the dance floor set several dancers on fire. This woman ruled France during the madness of her husband, Charles VI, and her historical reputation suffered due to polemics exchanged in the Burgundian-Armagnac Dispute.

clues.clue: Isabeau of Bavaria's reputation suffered due to polemics exchanged during the Burgundian-Armagnac civil war, which broke out amid Charles VI's incapacity
examples: This woman ruled France during the madness of her husband, Charles VI, and her historical reputation suffered due to polemics exchanged in the Burgundian-Armagnac Dispute.

works.name: End of the War: Castillon & the Treaty of Picquigny
works.description: Near the war's end, Charles VII "the Victorious" captured Bordeaux and defeated the English commander John Talbot (previously captured at Patay) at the Battle of Castillon (1453), where Jean Bureau's use of a fortified camp with ditches and cannon helped end English dominance and unofficially concluded the war, leaving England only the Pale of Calais on the continent. The war was formally ended more than two decades later when Louis XI "the Spider King" defeated Charles the Bold of Burgundy and signed the Treaty of Picquigny (1475) with England.

clues.clue: Near the war's end, Charles VII "the Victorious" captured Bordeaux and defeated the English at Castillon; commander John Talbot, previously captured at Patay, was killed in the battle
examples: A king of this name was nicknamed "the Victorious" after his armies captured Bordeaux and defeated the English at Castillon at the end of the Hundred Years War.
Near the end of this war, John Talbot was defeated at the Battle of Castillon.
In one of its final battles, a commander previously captured at Patay was killed.

clues.clue: At Castillon, Jean Bureau set up a fortified camp with a ditch and cannon, helping to reduce the effectiveness of the longbow and end English dominance
examples: That battle in this war was won when Jean Bureau set up a ditch and cannon around his camp near Castillon.
The efficacy of the longbow was reduced by the use of this other weapon, which the French used in 1453 at Castillon.

clues.clue: The English defeat at Castillon unofficially ended the war, leaving England with only the Pale of Calais as a continental holding
examples: Henry also lost this war against the French during its final Lancastrian Phase. The English defeat at Castillon unofficially ended this war and left England with the Pale of Calais as its only major continental holding.

clues.clue: The war was formally ended in 1475 when Louis XI "the Spider King" defeated Charles the Bold of Burgundy and signed the Treaty of Picquigny with England
examples: This monarch defeated Charles the Bold of Burgundy and signed the Treaty of Picquigny, formally ending the Hundred Years War. This monarch gained his arachnid epithet for his cunning.
This conflict's last stage saw John Talbot lose Gascony after the Battle of Castillon, twenty years before the signing of the Treaty of Picquigny.

comprehensive_summary: The Hundred Years' War (1337-1453) was a long series of conflicts between England and France, whose history is chiefly recorded in Jean Froissart's Chronicles and whose tide was eventually turned by the intervention of Joan of Arc. The war grew out of a succession dispute after the senior line of the Capetian dynasty failed to produce a male heir: Edward III of England, whose claim came through his mother Isabella of France (a former lover of Roger Mortimer), disputed the throne with Philip VI of Valois amid confusion over Salic law, and Philip VI's confiscation of English-held Aquitaine helped spark open war, which formally began with the English naval victory at the Battle of Sluys (1340). At the Battle of Crecy (1346), Edward the Black Prince helped defeat the French and their hired Genoese crossbowmen, killing the blind King John of Bohemia, whose three-ostrich-feather crest the English victor adopted; a decade later at the Battle of Poitiers (1356), the Black Prince captured King John II "the Good," whose ransom under the humiliating Treaty of Bretigny (three million ecus, 40 hostages, and territories including Guyenne and Gascony) ended the war's Edwardian phase and sparked the political instability behind the Jacquerie, a peasant revolt led by Guillaume Cale ("Jack Goodfellow") that was crushed by Charles the Bad of Navarre at the Battle of Mello. A related conflict, the War of the Breton Succession between the Houses of Blois and Montfort, was decided in the Montforts' favor (with English aid from Sir John Chandos) at the Battle of Auray, and featured the chivalric Combat of the Thirty. The war's middle years were marked by devastating chevauchee raids and by demobilized mercenary "free companies" like the Archpriest's and Rodrigo de Villandrando's "Flayers of Dead Men," as well as by figures such as condottiere John Hawkwood's White Company, knight Geoffroi de Charny (author of the Book of Chivalry), the future serial killer Gilles de Rais, and the judicial duel between Jean de Carrouges and Jacques le Gris. King Charles V "the Wise" won a naval victory at La Rochelle with his constable Bertrand du Guesclin, while his son Charles VI "the Mad," who believed he was made of glass, saw his queen Isabeau of Bavaria's reputation damaged by the Burgundian-Armagnac civil war and a masquerade ball fire (the Bal des Ardents). At the Battle of Agincourt (1415), fought on Saint Crispin's Day and later dramatized by Shakespeare, Henry V's English and Welsh longbowmen destroyed Charles d'Albret's cavalry after a charge across muddy terrain, a triumph followed by Henry's marriage to Catherine of Valois and the 1420 Treaty of Troyes. In 1429, the peasant girl Joan of Arc led the French army in lifting the Siege of Orleans, helping crown Charles VII at Reims before being captured by Burgundian forces and burned at the stake for cross-dressing; her intervention turned the war's tide, and Charles VII "the Victorious" went on to capture Bordeaux and defeat the English commander John Talbot at the Battle of Castillon (1453), where Jean Bureau's fortified cannon position helped end English dominance and unofficially conclude the war, leaving England only the Pale of Calais. The conflict was formally ended in 1475 when Louis XI "the Spider King" defeated Charles the Bold of Burgundy and signed the Treaty of Picquigny with England.
recursive_suggestions: Joan of Arc — appeared as a recurring figure with dense independent detail (trial, Beaurevoir escape attempt, De Quadam Puella) that likely already supports, or should support, her own standalone page
Battle of Agincourt — recurs heavily with Shakespearean/literary crossover detail; check for a standalone answerline treatment, possibly split History vs. Literature
The Jacquerie — appeared as its own answerline with substantial independent detail (Guillaume Cale, Battle of Mello, Charles the Bad) that could support a standalone page
War of the Breton Succession — appeared as its own answerline with detail (Battle of Auray, Combat of the Thirty, Sir John Chandos) that could support a standalone page
Jean Froissart — recurring chronicler figure; check for a standalone Literature/History page on his Chronicles
Gilles de Rais — recurring as a serial-killer figure connected to Joan of Arc's circle; likely has enough independent detail (Bluebeard inspiration, trial) for his own page
Charles VI of France / the Mad — recurring figure (glass delusion, Bal des Ardents) with enough detail for a standalone Ruler page
Duke of Burgundy (Philip the Good / Philip the Bold) — recurring across Burgundian-Armagnac civil war content; check for a standalone Ruler page distinct from this War page
